items.title: Company and chart-of-accounts setup scenario
items.description: A foundational end-to-end scenario that provisions a company and its chart of accounts so every later scenario has a valid posting context. It creates the company, seeds the typed account tree and confirms postable leaf accounts exist before any transaction is attempted.
items.applicability: The setup phase that every other scenario in a generated ERP depends on.
items.example: The scenario creates 'Acme Ltd', seeds Assets/Liabilities/Equity/Income/Expense roots with leaf accounts (Bank, Sales, Purchases), and asserts a journal can target a leaf but not a group.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: make setup idempotent or isolated per test run so scenarios do not collide on the same company. Test implication: assert the company and a minimal postable chart exist before proceeding. This scenario proves the bootstrap path works; it does not validate a complete, jurisdiction-specific chart of accounts.

items.title: Quote-to-cash scenario
items.description: An end-to-end sales scenario covering quotation through sales order, delivery, sales invoice and customer receipt, asserting that each step posts correctly and the customer balance and revenue accounts reflect the cycle. It proves the selling flow integrates with the GL and AR control accounts.
items.applicability: Validation of the order-to-cash flow in a generated ERP with selling and accounts-receivable modules.
items.example: The scenario raises a quote, converts it to an order, delivers stock, invoices the customer, receives payment, and asserts revenue is recognised and the AR balance returns to zero.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: drive each step through the real submit path so GL postings are exercised, not stubbed. Test implication: assert revenue, AR and cash balances at each milestone. The scenario validates the implemented happy path and its postings; it does not cover every pricing, discount, tax or returns variation.

items.title: Procure-to-pay scenario
items.description: An end-to-end purchasing scenario covering purchase order through goods receipt, purchase invoice and supplier payment, asserting the AP control account and expense or inventory accounts move correctly. It proves the buying flow integrates with the GL and AP subledger.
items.applicability: Validation of the procure-to-pay flow in a generated ERP with buying and accounts-payable modules.
items.example: The scenario raises a PO, receives goods, books a purchase invoice, pays the supplier, and asserts the AP balance clears and inventory or expense is recognised.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: exercise goods receipt and invoice as distinct posting steps so the GRNI/AP interplay is tested. Test implication: assert AP and inventory/expense balances at each step. The scenario covers the implemented straight-through purchase; partial receipts, landed costs and complex tax handling are out of its scope.

items.title: Inventory receipt, issue, transfer and adjustment scenario
items.description: An end-to-end stock scenario exercising the four stock movements — receipt into a warehouse, issue out, transfer between warehouses, and adjustment for count differences — and asserting that quantities and stock valuation update consistently with the stock ledger.
items.applicability: Validation of the inventory module's movement postings in a generated ERP.
items.example: The scenario receives 100 units, issues 30, transfers 20 to a second warehouse, adjusts +2 on a recount, and asserts per-warehouse quantities and total valuation reconcile to the stock ledger.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: route every movement through the stock-posting service so valuation stays consistent. Test implication: assert quantity and value after each movement type. The scenario validates the implemented valuation method on these movements; it does not cover every costing method, serial/batch tracking depth, or manufacturing consumption.

items.title: Journal approval scenario
items.description: An end-to-end scenario for a manual journal entry through its approval workflow: a maker creates and submits a balanced journal, a checker approves it, and the GL reflects the posting only after approval. It proves the workflow gates posting on approval rather than on submission alone.
items.applicability: Validation of the journal approval workflow in a generated ERP with maker-checker controls.
items.example: A clerk submits a balanced journal which sits pending; a manager approves it; the scenario asserts the GL rows appear only after approval and the document status becomes approved.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: ensure the GL post fires on approval, not submission, so a pending journal does not affect balances. Test implication: assert no GL impact before approval and the correct impact after. The scenario covers single-level approval; multi-level or conditional approval chains, if added, need their own scenarios.

items.title: Month-end trial-balance, P&L and balance-sheet check scenario
items.description: An end-to-end period-close scenario that, after a set of transactions, runs the trial balance, profit-and-loss and balance-sheet and asserts they are internally consistent: the trial balance nets to zero, and assets equal liabilities plus equity including the period's profit. It proves the reporting layer reconciles with the ledger.
items.applicability: Validation of the financial-statement reporting in a generated ERP at period close.
items.example: After running the quote-to-cash and procure-to-pay scenarios, the close scenario asserts total debits equal total credits, and the balance sheet balances once net profit flows into equity.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: derive all three statements from the same GL so they cannot disagree. Test implication: assert the trial balance nets to zero and the accounting equation holds. The scenario checks arithmetic consistency of the implemented reports; it does not assert conformance to a specific accounting standard's presentation.

items.title: RBAC-negative scenario: a user without the role is blocked
items.description: A negative end-to-end scenario in which a user lacking the required RolePermission attempts a protected operation and is denied. It proves the access-control matrix actually blocks unauthorised actions through the same path a real user would take, not just permits authorised ones.
items.applicability: Validation of role-based access control in a generated ERP.
items.example: A user with only read permission on invoices attempts to submit one; the scenario asserts the operation is forbidden and no document or GL change occurs.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: run the denied action through the real authorisation boundary so the block is genuine. Test implication: assert both the denial and the absence of side effects. A negative scenario proves the tested action is blocked; it cannot enumerate every unauthorised path, so broad RBAC coverage needs many such cases.

items.title: Maker/checker-negative scenario: the maker cannot approve their own document
items.description: A negative end-to-end scenario in which the user who submitted a document attempts to approve it themselves and is refused. It proves the segregation-of-duties control holds end to end, blocking unilateral action even by an otherwise authorised approver who happens to be the maker.
items.applicability: Validation of maker-checker segregation in a generated ERP.
items.example: A manager who is also an approver submits a payment, then tries to approve it; the scenario asserts the self-approval is refused and the document stays pending for another checker.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: compare approver to recorded maker inside the approval path so the refusal cannot be bypassed. Test implication: assert self-approval fails and a different checker succeeds. The scenario proves the maker-checker rule for the modelled flow; collusion between two distinct users is a process risk the control cannot address.

items.title: Audit-trail assertion scenario
items.description: An end-to-end scenario that performs a sequence of actions and then asserts the audit trail captured each one with the correct actor, UTC time and entity, and that the trail cannot be altered. It proves the audit subsystem records the right events immutably rather than assuming it does.
items.applicability: Validation of the audit subsystem in a generated ERP.
items.example: After creating, submitting and approving a document, the scenario asserts three audit rows with the expected actors and actions exist and that an attempt to edit one fails.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: read the audit rows back through a query and compare to the actions taken. Test implication: assert one audit row per event with correct stamps and that mutation is refused. The scenario verifies the application's audit behaviour; it cannot prove immutability against direct database access, which is governed elsewhere.

items.title: Scenarios prove the implemented engine, not full ERPNext breadth
items.description: The scenario library demonstrates that the implemented accounting, inventory, workflow and control engine behaves correctly end to end. It deliberately does not attempt to reproduce the full business breadth of a mature commercial ERP; the scenarios are correctness proofs for what is built, stated honestly as such.
items.applicability: Setting expectations about what the scenario suite of a generated ERP does and does not certify.
items.example: Release notes state 'scenarios validate the implemented engine (GL, stock, workflow, controls); they are not a claim of full ERPNext feature parity', so a reviewer reads them as correctness evidence, not breadth evidence.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: keep scenarios aligned to actually-implemented features so a passing suite never implies unbuilt functionality. Test implication: each scenario maps to a real implemented flow. The honesty boundary means stakeholders must not infer feature completeness from a green scenario suite; unimplemented business processes remain unproven and unbuilt.
